Comments: Can newer built houses roof and walls sustain category 4 hurricane? Should those in the path or near the eye wall evacuate if they are not in the zone for evacuation for floods. How worried should we be for wind speeds for category 4 that our roof and house will fall apart (houses built in 2017)

Greg Dee on ABC news just reported that if you are in a well built home and sheltering in place, you will be ok. It’s the surge from the wind that causes destruction. He said you may have some damage to your roof and such but you will be ok. That made me feel a little better!

keeping all internal doors closed - so set up camp & stay in one - two rooms is best, and resist all temptation to have a peek outside until the storm has passed (via social media and radio updates). Good luck x

they should. Just have a plan. As I recall people got in bathtubs and put their mattresses over them in Homestead in 92. We l8ved in an older house with lath and plaster ceilings so when our roof blew off it held until the next day. Of course it was raining in the entire house in lines 16" apart. We had boarded everything so no windows blew. We all huddled in an inside room. I remember hearing loud bing, bing,Bing as the roofing nails started ripping out. So again, have a plan that everyone knows, have a clear path to your place and execute it as fast as possible. God speed.

Building Codes: Homes built in 2017, especially in hurricane-prone areas, should comply with local building codes designed to withstand wind speeds of 130-156 mph, which is the range for a Category 4 hurricane. Roofs, walls, windows, and doors are often built to be more resistant to wind.
if you have any problems with your roof, if needed, we have crews on standby to tarp homes as needed. Here to help . Hope you and your family stay safe during this storm.
